text: Which among the following is the most common reaction type in alkyl halides?
explanation:
Alkyl halides readily undergo **nucleophilic substitution** reactions (SN1 and SN2).

In these reactions:
- The carbon bearing the halogen is **electrophilic** (őī+) due to the electronegative halogen
- A stronger nucleophile (Nu‚ĀĽ) **replaces** the weaker nucleophile (leaving group X‚ĀĽ)
- General reaction: $$\text{R-X} + \text{Nu}^- \rightarrow \text{R-Nu} + \text{X}^-$$

This is why alkyl halides are such important intermediates in organic synthesis ‚ÄĒ they readily react with a wide variety of nucleophiles (OH‚ĀĽ, CN‚ĀĽ, NH‚āÉ, RO‚ĀĽ, etc.).
name: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes Item 1

text: The C‚ÄďCl bond in chlorobenzene compared to the C‚ÄďCl bond in methyl chloride is:
explanation:
In **chlorobenzene**, the Cl atom has a **+R (resonance) effect** ‚ÄĒ its lone pairs are donated into the ŌÄ-system of the benzene ring:

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{-Cl} \leftrightarrow [\text{resonance structures with C=Cl character}]$$

This gives the C‚ÄďCl bond **partial double bond character**, which makes it:
- **Shorter** than a pure C‚ÄďCl single bond (bond length ~1.69 √Ö vs ~1.77 √Ö in CH‚āÉCl)
- **Stronger** due to partial double bond character

In **methyl chloride (CH‚āÉCl)**, the Cl is attached to sp¬≥ carbon with no resonance ‚ÄĒ it's a pure single bond, longer and weaker.

This also explains why chlorobenzene is **less reactive** towards nucleophilic substitution than alkyl halides.
name: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes Item 2

text: When benzyl chloride undergoes chlorination with Cl‚āā/FeCl‚āÉ, the major product A formed is:
explanation:
In benzyl chloride (C‚āÜH‚āÖCH‚āāCl), the ‚ÄďCH‚āāCl group is an electron-withdrawing group through induction (‚ąíI effect), but the ring itself undergoes electrophilic aromatic substitution.

However, the ‚ÄďCH‚āāCl group has a **weak +R effect** ‚ÄĒ making it an **ortho/para director** (weak activator).

When benzyl chloride is treated with Cl‚āā/FeCl‚āÉ:
- Cl‚Āļ (electrophile) attacks the benzene ring
- **Ortho and para** substitution occurs predominantly

Major product **A = mixture of ortho-chlorobenzyl chloride and para-chlorobenzyl chloride**
name: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes Item 3

text: Which among the following statements is TRUE about the reactivity of haloarenes towards nucleophilic substitution reactions?
options: Haloarenes are very reactive towards nucleophilic substitution reactions
In haloarenes, the C‚ÄďX bond acquires partial double bond character
The C‚ÄďX bond in haloarenes is longer than the C‚ÄďX bond in haloalkanes
In haloarenes, the phenyl cation formed as a result of self-ionisation is stable
explanation:
**Statement 2 is correct:** Due to the **+R (resonance) effect** of the halogen atom, the lone pairs of X are delocalized into the benzene ring, giving the C‚ÄďX bond in haloarenes **partial double bond character**.

Consequences:
- The C‚ÄďX bond becomes **shorter** than in haloalkanes (not longer)
- The bond becomes **stronger** ‚ÄĒ harder to break
- Nucleophilic substitution becomes **difficult** (haloarenes are less reactive than haloalkanes towards SN)

**Statements 1, 3, 4 are incorrect:**
- Haloarenes are **less reactive** (not more reactive) towards nucleophilic substitution
- C‚ÄďX in haloarenes is **shorter** (not longer) than in haloalkanes
- Phenyl cations (sp-hybridised) are **very unstable**
name: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes Item 4

text:
The increasing order of densities of the following alkyl halides is:

I. n-C‚āÉH‚āáBr   II. n-C‚āÉH‚āáCl   III. n-C‚āÉH‚āáI
explanation:
The density of alkyl halides increases with increasing molecular mass and atomic mass of the halogen.

For the same alkyl group (n-propyl), the halogen determines density:

| Compound | Halogen | Atomic mass of X | Density |
|---|---|---|---|
| n-C‚āÉH‚āáCl (II) | Cl | 35.5 | ~0.89 g/mL |
| n-C‚āÉH‚āáBr (I) | Br | 80 | ~1.335 g/mL |
| n-C‚āÉH‚āáI (III) | I | 127 | ~1.747 g/mL |

**Increasing order of density: II < I < III**
(n-C‚āÉH‚āáCl < n-C‚āÉH‚āáBr < n-C‚āÉH‚āáI)
name: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes Item 5

text: Which among the following compounds is a gem-dihalide?
explanation:
**Gem-dihalides** (geminal dihalides) are compounds where **two halogen atoms are on the same carbon**.

- **Ethylidene chloride** (CH‚āÉ‚ÄďCHCl‚āā): Both Cl atoms on **C2** ‚Üí **gem-dihalide** ‚úď

- **Ethylene dichloride** (ClCH‚āā‚ÄďCH‚āāCl): One Cl on C1 and one on C2 ‚Üí **vicinal dihalide** ‚úó

- **Methyl chloride** (CH‚āÉCl): Only one halogen ‚Üí **monohalide** ‚úó

- **Benzyl chloride** (C‚āÜH‚āÖCH‚āāCl): Only one halogen ‚Üí **monohalide** ‚úó

Note: The other type of dihalide is **vicinal (vic-)** where halogens are on adjacent carbons.
name: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes Item 6

text:
For the reaction: $$\text{CH}_2\text{=CH}_2 + \text{Br}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{NaCl(aq)}} \text{Products}$$

Which of the following is NOT a possible product?
explanation:
When ethylene reacts with Br‚āā in aqueous NaCl, the mechanism involves:

**Step 1:** Br‚āā acts as electrophile ‚Üí forms a **bromonium ion** intermediate (cyclic)

**Step 2:** The bromonium ion is attacked by available **nucleophiles** from the solution:
- **Br‚ĀĽ** ‚Üí gives 1,2-dibromoethane (BrCH‚āāCH‚āāBr) ‚úď
- **Cl‚ĀĽ** (from NaCl) ‚Üí gives BrCH‚āāCH‚āāCl ‚úď
- **H‚āāO** (solvent) ‚Üí gives BrCH‚āāCH‚āāOH ‚úď

**ClCH‚āāCH‚āāCl is NOT possible** because Cl‚āā was never added ‚ÄĒ there is no source of the Cl‚Āļ electrophile needed to form a chloronium ion. Only Br‚āā acts as the electrophile, so one end must always be Br.

Therefore, **Cl‚ÄďCH‚āā‚ÄďCH‚āā‚ÄďCl cannot form** in this reaction.
name: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes Item 7

text:
Arrange the following organic halides in correct order of reactivity towards SN2 displacement:

P: $$(\text{CH}_3)_3\text{C-Br}$$   Q: $$\text{BrCH}_2(\text{CH}_2)_2\text{CH}_3$$   R: $$\text{CH}_3\text{CH(Br)(CH}_2\text{CH}_3)$$
explanation:
In **SN2 reactions**, the rate is inversely proportional to **steric hindrance** at the carbon being attacked.

**Identifying the halides:**
- **P:** $$(\text{CH}_3)_3\text{C-Br}$$ = **Tertiary (3¬į) alkyl halide** ‚ÄĒ maximum steric hindrance ‚Üí slowest SN2
- **Q:** $$\text{BrCH}_2(\text{CH}_2)_2\text{CH}_3$$ = **Primary (1¬į) alkyl halide** ‚ÄĒ minimum steric hindrance ‚Üí fastest SN2
- **R:** $$\text{CH}_3\text{CH(Br)CH}_2\text{CH}_3$$ = **Secondary (2¬į) alkyl halide** ‚ÄĒ intermediate steric hindrance

**SN2 rate order:** 1¬į > 2¬į > 3¬į

**Therefore: Q > R > P**
name: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes Item 8

text:
Which of the following statements about chlorobenzene are correct?

A. The C‚ÄďCl bond in chlorobenzene is shorter than in chloromethane.
B. It is more difficult to replace chlorine from chlorobenzene than from benzyl chloride.
C. The C‚ÄďCl bond in chlorobenzene has some double bond character.
D. Chlorobenzene on chlorination gives m-dichlorobenzene.
explanation:
**Statement A ‚ÄĒ Correct:** Due to partial double bond character (from +R effect of Cl), the C‚ÄďCl bond in chlorobenzene is **shorter** (~1.69 √Ö) than in chloromethane (~1.77 √Ö). ‚úď

**Statement B ‚ÄĒ Correct:** The partial double bond character makes the C‚ÄďCl bond stronger in chlorobenzene ‚Üí harder to break ‚Üí **more difficult** to replace Cl compared to benzyl chloride (where Cl is on a benzylic sp¬≥ carbon). ‚úď

**Statement C ‚ÄĒ Correct:** Due to +R effect, lone pairs of Cl delocalize into the ring ‚Üí C‚ÄďCl has **partial double bond character**. ‚úď

**Statement D ‚ÄĒ Incorrect:** Chlorobenzene on chlorination gives **ortho and para** dichlorobenzenes (not meta), because ‚ÄďCl is an **ortho/para director** due to its +R effect. ‚úó

Correct statements: **A, B, C**
name: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes Item 9

text:
What are X, Y and Z in the following reactions?

$$\text{(CH}_3)_3\text{CONa} + \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{Br} \rightarrow X + \text{NaBr}$$

$$\text{(CH}_3)_3\text{CBr} + \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{ONa} \rightarrow Y + Z$$
explanation:
**Williamson Synthesis** ‚ÄĒ the product depends on the type of halide and alkoxide.

**Reaction 1:** **(CH‚āÉ)‚āÉCO‚ĀĽNa‚Āļ** (3¬į alkoxide, bulky) + **CH‚āÉCH‚āāBr** (1¬į halide, less hindered):
- 3¬į alkoxide + 1¬į halide ‚Üí **SN2 proceeds** ‚Üí **ether formed**
$$\text{(CH}_3)_3\text{CONa} + \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{Br} \rightarrow \textbf{X = (CH}_3)_3\text{COCH}_2\text{CH}_3 + \text{NaBr}$$

**Reaction 2:** **(CH‚āÉ)‚āÉCBr** (3¬į halide, highly hindered) + **CH‚āÉCH‚āāO‚ĀĽNa‚Āļ** (1¬į alkoxide):
- 3¬į halide + any base ‚Üí **E2 elimination** (SN2 blocked by steric hindrance)
$$\text{(CH}_3)_3\text{CBr} + \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{ONa} \rightarrow \textbf{Y = CH}_2\text{=C(CH}_3)_2 + \textbf{Z = CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH} + \text{NaBr}$$
name: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes Item 10

text:
**Assertion (A):** Propene on addition with hydrogen bromide in the presence of peroxide gives 1-bromopropane as the major product.

**Reason (R):** 1-Bromopropane is the major product because it is formed through the stable carbocation.

The correct answer is:
options: (A) and (R) are correct, (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
(A) and (R) are correct but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
(A) is correct but (R) is not correct
(A) is not correct but (R) is correct
explanation:
**Assertion (A) is CORRECT:**
In the presence of peroxide, HBr addition to propene follows **anti-Markovnikov's rule** (free radical mechanism):
$$\text{CH}_3\text{-CH=CH}_2 + \text{HBr} \xrightarrow{\text{peroxide}} \text{CH}_3\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{Br}$$
Major product = **1-bromopropane** ‚úď

**Reason (R) is INCORRECT:**
The free radical mechanism does **NOT involve carbocations**. Instead:
- HBr generates a **Br‚ÄĘ radical** (initiation step)
- Br‚ÄĘ adds to the less substituted end of the double bond (forming the more stable **secondary radical**)
- The secondary radical then reacts with HBr to give 1-bromopropane

So (A) is correct but (R) incorrectly mentions carbocations ‚ÄĒ the free radical mechanism involves **radicals, not carbocations**.
name: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes Item 11

text: Identify the halogen exchange reaction from the following:
explanation:
**Finkelstein reaction** is the halogen exchange reaction:

$$\text{R-Cl (or R-Br)} + \text{NaI} \xrightarrow{\text{acetone}} \text{R-I} + \text{NaCl (or NaBr)}\downarrow$$

- It is an **SN2 reaction** (backside attack)
- Used to convert alkyl chlorides or bromides into **alkyl iodides**
- Driven by the **insolubility of NaCl/NaBr** in acetone (Le Chatelier's principle)

Other reactions:
- **Sandmeyer:** Converts diazonium salts to aryl halides using CuCl/CuBr
- **Fittig:** Couples aryl halides using Na in dry ether
- **Wurtz-Fittig:** Couples alkyl + aryl halides using Na
name: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes Item 12

text: The rate of dehydrohalogenation (E2 elimination) of which one among the following is least?
explanation:
The rate of dehydrohalogenation (E2 elimination) depends on:

1. **Type of alkyl halide:** 3¬į > 2¬į > 1¬į
2. **Nature of halogen (leaving group ability):** I > Br > Cl > F

Among the given options:
- All of (a), (b), (c) are **primary alkyl halides** of similar chain length
- **(d)** is also primary but on a branched chain

For the **halogens** in the primary propyl halides:
- n-C‚āÉH‚āáI (c): Best leaving group (I‚ĀĽ) ‚Üí fastest
- n-C‚āÉH‚āáBr (a): Br‚ĀĽ is intermediate leaving group
- n-C‚āÉH‚āáCl (b): Cl‚ĀĽ is the **worst leaving group** among the halogens listed ‚Üí **slowest E2 rate**

Therefore, **CH‚āÉCH‚āāCH‚āāCl has the least rate** of dehydrohalogenation.
name: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes Item 13

text: Propyne reacts with HBr to form compound Z. The compound Z is:
explanation:
Propyne reacts with **2 moles of HBr** via electrophilic addition following **Markovnikov's rule** at each step:

**Step 1:** First HBr addition:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{-C‚Č°CH} + \text{HBr} \xrightarrow{\text{Markovnikov}} \text{CH}_3\text{-CBr=CH}_2$$
(H adds to terminal C, Br to internal C)

**Step 2:** Second HBr addition:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{-CBr=CH}_2 + \text{HBr} \xrightarrow{\text{Markovnikov}} \text{CH}_3\text{-CBr}_2\text{-CH}_3$$
(H adds to CH‚āā, Br adds to CBr carbon)

**Product Z = 2,2-dibromopropane (CH‚āÉCBr‚āāCH‚āÉ)**

This is a **gem-dihalide** where both Br atoms are on the same (middle) carbon.
name: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes Item 14

text: SN2 reaction involving inversion of configuration takes place with an optically active compound Z. The compound Z is:
explanation:
For **SN2 with inversion of configuration**, the compound must:
1. Be **optically active** (have a chiral center)
2. Be reactive towards SN2 (not too sterically hindered)

Analysing the options:
- **(a) CH‚āÉCH‚āāX:** Primary ‚ÄĒ no chiral center ‚Üí **not optically active** ‚úó
- **(b) (CH‚āÉ)‚āāCHX:** Secondary ‚ÄĒ no chiral center (both groups on either side of CHX are identical) ‚Üí **not optically active** ‚úó
- **(c) CH‚āÉCH‚āāCH(CH‚āÉ)X:** Secondary ‚ÄĒ **has a chiral center** (C bearing X has 4 different groups: H, CH‚āÉ, C‚āāH‚āÖ, X) ‚Üí **optically active** ‚úď, and 2¬į halides undergo SN2 ‚úď
- **(d) (CH‚āÉ)‚āÉCX:** Tertiary ‚ÄĒ no chiral center AND too hindered for SN2 ‚úó

**Compound Z = CH‚āÉCH‚āāCH(CH‚āÉ)X**
name: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes Item 15

text: Which one of the following has the highest dipole moment?
explanation:
**Dipole moment** depends on the **vector sum of individual bond dipoles**.

- **(a) cis-but-2-ene:** The two CH‚āÉ groups have small bond moments; they partially reinforce each other ‚Üí **small dipole moment (~0.33 D)**

- **(b) trans-1,2-dichloroethene:** The two C‚ÄďCl bond dipoles point in **opposite directions** ‚Üí they **cancel out completely** ‚Üí **őľ = 0 D**

- **(c) cis-1,2-dichloroethene:** The two C‚ÄďCl bond dipoles point in the **same direction** ‚Üí they **add up** ‚Üí **őľ = 1.89 D** (highest) ‚úď

- **(d) trans-but-2-ene:** The two CH‚āÉ groups point in opposite directions ‚Üí dipole moments cancel ‚Üí **őľ = 0 D**

**cis-1,2-dichloroethene has the highest dipole moment (1.89 D)**
name: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes Item 16
